title: Yoga and Kundalini Awakening: Why Some Practices Accelerate the Process

body:
Yoga and Kundalini Awakening: Why Some Practices Accelerate the Process

Hey. You're probably reading this because something's happening. Something big, scary, and you don't know who to talk to. Maybe you've been doing yoga, meditating, trying to 'better yourself,' and now you're feeling things you never imagined. Sensations, energy, fear. You're wondering if you're going crazy, right? I've been there. I know that 3 AM dread, the feeling like your body's betraying you, your mind's unraveling. It's not a joke. It's real, and it's terrifying when you don't have a map.

Look, I'm not here to tell you everything's going to be okay in some fluffy, spiritual-teacher way. I'm here to tell you what I've seen, what I've lived, and what I've learned from others who walked through the fire. You're not alone. What you're experiencing, this intense energetic activation, often gets called 'kundalini awakening.' And yeah, certain yoga practices, especially the strong stuff, can definitely kickstart it. Or, more accurately, they can throw gasoline on a fire that was already smoldering.

You probably got into yoga for peace, flexibility, maybe to feel a bit more grounded. You weren't signing up for this cosmic rollercoaster, were you? Nobody tells you about this part in the shiny studios with the perfect lighting and the Lululemon. They talk about chakras, sure, but not about the possibility of them feeling like they're exploding, or that energy could feel like it's ripping through your spine. But here's the thing: it happens. And it often happens to people who are dedicated, who are really trying to push their boundaries in their practice.

The Energetic Body: More Than Just Muscles

You know, in the West, we're taught to think of the body as this mechanical thing. Muscles, bones, organs. But ancient traditions, like yoga, they've always seen it differently. They talk about an energetic body, a subtle body. It's not something you can see on an X-ray, but you can sure as hell feel it when it's active. This is where the concept of prana comes in - life force energy. And then there's nadis, which are like energetic channels, and chakras, which are these spinning wheels of energy along your spine. They're not metaphors; they're considered real aspects of your being in these systems.

When you're doing intense yoga, you're not just stretching your hamstrings. You're working with this subtle system. You're manipulating prana. You're directing energy. And sometimes, you're doing it in ways that your system isn't quite ready for, or that open up channels that were better left closed for a while. Think of it like this: you've got a really old house with old wiring. You can slowly upgrade it, or you can plug in a super-high-voltage appliance and blow the fuses. Some yoga practices are like that super-high-voltage appliance.

I've seen it myself. I've been in workshops where the energy was so thick you could taste it. People shaking, crying, laughing uncontrollably. Teachers who were pushing, pushing, pushing, without really understanding the potential consequences for everyone in the room. You go in looking for peace, and you come out feeling like you've been plugged into a wall socket. Know what I mean? It's disorienting. It makes you question everything you thought you knew about yourself and reality.

Yoga Practices That Can Be Triggers

So, what kind of yoga are we talking about here? It's not your gentle restorative class, that's for sure. It's usually the more intense, energy-focused, or breath-heavy practices. You're probably familiar with some of these, or maybe you've been doing them without realizing their deeper energetic impact.

  • Kundalini Yoga (the specific lineage): This one's in the name, right? It's designed to awaken kundalini. It uses a lot of repetitive movements, strong breathwork (pranayama), chanting, and meditations. It's powerful stuff. If you've been doing this, and you're feeling intense energy, you're not imagining it. That's what it's for. But it doesn't always go smoothly. Sometimes it's like opening Pandora's Box without a clear instruction manual on how to close it, or how to deal with what flies out.
  • Intense Pranayama (Breathwork): Practices like Bhastrika (bellows breath), Kapalabhati (skull shining breath), or retention practices (kumbhaka) can seriously move energy. When you hold your breath, you're building up pressure, both physical and energetic. When you release it, that energy has to go somewhere. If your system isn't prepared, if your nadis aren't clear, it can feel like an explosion. It can stir up old traumas, bring intense heat, vibrations, or even visions. It's not just about getting more oxygen to your brain, trust me.
  • Vigorous Asana (Physical Postures): While not as direct as pranayama, certain intense asana practices, especially those held for a long time or done in a very dynamic, repetitive way, can also get things moving. Think about long inversions, deep backbends, or practices that generate a lot of internal heat. They can create pressure, open channels, and direct prana. If you're doing a practice that leaves you feeling buzzy, shaky, or emotionally raw for days afterward, it's not just muscle soreness.
  • Chanting and Mantra Repetition: Sound is vibration. Repetitive chanting, especially with focus and intention, can create powerful energetic shifts. It can activate chakras, clear blockages, and build up a lot of subtle energy. If you've ever felt a strong vibration in your chest or head after chanting, you're feeling that energy directly.
  • Deep Meditation and Contemplation: While not strictly yoga asana, many yoga traditions include meditation. Extended periods of deep meditation, especially those focused on inner energy or specific chakras, can absolutely trigger kundalini activity. When you quiet the mind, you become much more aware of the subtle energies within you. And sometimes, that awareness itself is enough to kick things off.

You see, it's not just one thing. It's often a combination. You might be doing intense pranayama, followed by vigorous asana, and then deep meditation. You're basically creating a perfect storm for energetic activation. And while some people sail through it with blissful experiences, others end up in a crisis. That's the part nobody talks about enough.

The Crisis: What It Feels Like

So, you're here because you're in it, or you feel it coming. What does a kundalini crisis feel like? It's not uniform, but there are common threads. And it's not fun. It's not a spiritual glow-up. It's often a breakdown before a breakthrough, and the breakdown part can feel like hell on earth.

  • Intense Physical Sensations: You might feel extreme heat or cold, vibrations, tingling, crawling sensations, electric shocks, spasms, or involuntary movements. Sometimes it feels like something is literally moving inside your spine, or pushing out from your head. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) goes into a lot of these physical manifestations, and it can be a relief just to read that someone else has felt them too.
  • Emotional Rollercoaster: You're suddenly feeling emotions you haven't felt since childhood, or ever. Intense fear, anxiety, depression, rage, grief. You might cry uncontrollably for no reason, or feel waves of bliss followed by deep despair. It's like your emotional thermostat is broken, and you're cycling through every human emotion at warp speed.
  • Mental Confusion and Disorientation: Your thoughts might race, you might have trouble concentrating, or you might feel like your mind is breaking apart. Paranoia, delusions, or a feeling of unreality can set in. You might hear voices or see things that aren't there. This is often the scariest part, because it makes you question your sanity.
  • Spiritual/Existential Distress: You might feel disconnected from everything, or like you're losing your sense of self. Old beliefs crumble, and you're left with a void. You might have intense spiritual experiences, visions, or a feeling of cosmic connection, but it's often mixed with terror and confusion. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a really good resource for understanding this aspect, especially the sense of being overwhelmed by the experience.
  • Sleep Disturbances: Insomnia is super common. You might feel wired, unable to sleep, or have extremely vivid dreams, nightmares, or lucid dreaming experiences that feel more real than waking life.

I remember lying awake for nights, feeling like my body was vibrating off the bed, my mind replaying every mistake I'd ever made, and my heart pounding like it was going to explode. I thought I was dying, or going insane. And the doctors, bless their hearts, they didn't know what to do with me. They just looked at me like I was making it up, or prescribed me meds that just numbed me out without addressing the core of what was happening. That's why you need to know this isn't just "stress." This is something else.

Why It Happens: The Energetic Explanation

So, why do these practices sometimes lead to crisis instead of bliss? It's not because you're doing something wrong, not necessarily. Often, it's about what you're stirring up. Imagine that subtle energy system I talked about earlier. Over a lifetime, we accumulate blockages in these channels and chakras. Trauma, unresolved emotions, stress, unhealthy habits - they all create energetic congestion.

When you start doing intense yoga or pranayama, you're basically forcing a powerful current of energy (prana) through these blocked channels. It's like trying to push a huge amount of water through a clogged pipe. What happens? Pressure builds up. The pipe might burst. Or, the gunk in the pipe gets dislodged and comes rushing out, making a huge mess before it clears. That's often what a kundalini crisis feels like - a forced clearing, a violent purification.

The kundalini energy itself - this dormant spiritual energy at the base of the spine - it's incredibly potent. When it starts to awaken, it seeks to rise up the central channel (sushumna nadi), clearing everything in its path. If that path is blocked, it can get stuck, or it can force its way through in a chaotic, uncontrolled manner. This is where the intense heat, the involuntary movements, the emotional purges come from. It's the system trying to rebalance, trying to clear itself out, but doing it in a way that's overwhelming for the individual. The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a fantastic resource for understanding these internal processes and why they can feel so disruptive.

Another aspect is the psychological component. As kundalini rises, it often brings up repressed memories, unresolved traumas, and deep-seated psychological patterns. It's like a cosmic therapist forcing you to confront every single thing you've ever pushed down. If you don't have a stable psychological foundation, or if you don't have good support systems, this can be incredibly destabilizing. Your ego, your sense of self, gets challenged in a very fundamental way. And that's terrifying. You're basically being deconstructed, and you don't know what's going to be left when it's over.

What To Do When You're In It

Okay, so you're feeling this. What now? First, breathe. Seriously. Just focus on your breath. It's the anchor. Don't panic, even though every cell in your body is screaming at you to. You're not going crazy, you're going through a process. A rough, messy, unpredictable process, but a process nonetheless.

Here's what I wish someone had told me:

  • Stop the Practices: If you're doing intense yoga, pranayama, or meditation that seems to be making it worse, STOP. Immediately. Don't push through. Don't think you need to do more to 'fix' it. You've already activated something; now you need to integrate it, not add more fuel to the fire.
  • Ground Yourself: This is crucial. Kundalini energy can make you feel ungrounded, like you're floating or disconnected. Focus on physical sensations. Eat grounding foods - root vegetables, heavy grains. Spend time in nature, with your bare feet on the earth. Do gentle, slow movements like walking or very gentle stretching. Avoid anything that makes you feel more 'up' or 'airy.'
  • Seek Support: This is not something to go through alone. Find someone who understands spiritual emergence or kundalini. It might be a therapist who specializes in transpersonal psychology, a spiritual counselor who's been through it, or a trusted friend who can just listen without judgment. You need someone who can hold space for you, who won't tell you you're crazy. The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is a classic for understanding these non-ordinary states and finding support.
  • Rest, Rest, Rest: Your system is working overtime. It's like you're running a marathon internally. Give yourself permission to rest. Sleep as much as you can. Don't try to force yourself to be productive or 'normal.'
  • Hydrate and Nourish: Drink plenty of water. Eat regular, nourishing meals. Your body needs fuel to process this energy. Avoid stimulants like caffeine and sugar, which can exacerbate anxiety and agitation.
  • Express Yourself: Don't keep it all bottled up. Journaling, talking to someone, creative expression - find a healthy outlet for the intense emotions and sensations you're experiencing.
  • Trust the Process (Eventually): This is the hardest part. When you're in the thick of it, it feels like it will never end. But it will. It's a purification. It's a shedding. It's your system recalibrating. It's incredibly uncomfortable, but there's a deeper intelligence at work. Your job right now is to ride the waves, not fight the ocean.

Honestly, you're going to feel like you're losing your mind. And in a way, you are. You're losing the old mind, the old ways of being. It's a death and rebirth process. It’s messy, it’s ugly, and it’s not always pretty. But if you can find the right support, and if you can learn to surrender to what's happening without resisting it every step of the way, you can come out the other side stronger, clearer, and more truly yourself than you've ever been.

It's a wild ride, and you didn't sign up for this part, I get it. But you're here now. Take it one breath at a time. You're tougher than you think.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
